title: Five Hand-Crafted Pillar Scenario Pages — Page Briefs
principle: Each page must answer: What does this page provide that the generic Loan Calculator page does not?

page_1_small_emergency_loan.search_intent: CALCULATOR — user wants to know the monthly payment for a small loan at a higher rate
page_1_small_emergency_loan.unique_use_case: Small emergency loans ($1K-$5K) carry higher rates because lenders have fixed costs. This page explains WHY small loans cost more and provides alternatives to high-rate small loans.
page_1_small_emergency_loan.why_this_page_deserves_to_exist: The generic Loan Calculator shows any payment, but it doesn't explain that $5K loans have higher APRs than $20K loans due to lender economics. This page provides context the parent calculator cannot: why small loans cost more, alternatives to high-rate lending, and when a small loan actually makes sense.
page_1_small_emergency_loan.alternative_scenario: Same $5,000 at 8% (excellent credit) = $156.68/mo, saving $588 in interest
page_1_small_emergency_loan.risk_warning: At 15% APR, you're paying nearly 25% extra on top of the principal. For small amounts, consider a credit union personal loan (often 8-12%) or a 0% APR credit card if eligible.
page_1_small_emergency_loan.comparison_angle: Compare 15% (fair credit) vs 8% (good credit) vs 25% (poor credit) for the same $5K amount. Show the real dollar cost of your credit score.
page_1_small_emergency_loan.doorway_defense: This is NOT a number-swap variant. It teaches WHY small loans cost more — unique educational content not found on generic calculator pages or other scenario pages.

secondary_queries: what credit score for 8 percent loan
page_2_good_credit_loan.search_intent: CALCULATOR — user wants to know the monthly payment for a $20K loan at a good rate
page_2_good_credit_loan.unique_use_case: The 'ideal' personal loan scenario — good credit, reasonable rate, medium amount. This is the benchmark page that other scenarios compare against.
page_2_good_credit_loan.why_this_page_deserves_to_exist: This is the GSC-validated query (114 impressions, pos 10.8). The existing $20K blog post ranks for this query but is an educational article, not a calculator-result page. This page serves the CALCULATOR intent, while the blog serves the EXPLANATORY intent. They are complementary, not competing.
page_2_good_credit_loan.alternative_scenario: Same $20,000 at 12% (average credit) = $444.89/mo — $2,362 more in interest over 5 years
page_2_good_credit_loan.risk_warning: Even at a 'good' 8% rate, you pay $4,332 in interest — that's 22% of the loan amount. Consider a shorter term (3 years) to reduce total interest, though monthly payments will be higher.
page_2_good_credit_loan.comparison_angle: Compare 8% vs 12% vs 20% for the same $20K amount. Show how much good credit saves you — $2,362 vs average credit, $7,134 vs poor credit.
internal_links: Sibling: fair-credit-loan-20000-20-percent (compare good vs poor credit)
page_2_good_credit_loan.doorway_defense: GSC-validated demand (114 imp). Serves calculator intent distinct from blog. Rich credit education content. Not a template — the benchmark page with unique comparisons.

secondary_queries: personal loan to pay off credit cards 25000
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.search_intent: CALCULATOR + COMPARISON — user wants to know if consolidating debt with a personal loan saves money vs credit cards
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.unique_use_case: Debt consolidation is the #1 reason people take personal loans. This page directly addresses the 'should I consolidate?' question with a comparison to credit card APR.
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.why_this_page_deserves_to_exist: The generic Loan Calculator doesn't compare loan rates to credit card rates. This page shows: 'If you're paying 25% APR on credit cards, a 10% consolidation loan saves you $X,XXX.' This is a decision-support page, not just a payment calculator.
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.alternative_scenario: If you kept $25K on credit cards at 25% APR and paid $415/mo: it would take 11+ years and cost $34,000+ in interest. The consolidation loan saves ~$24,000.
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.risk_warning: Consolidating debt only works if you stop using the credit cards. Many people consolidate, then run up new credit card balances — ending up with DOUBLE the debt. This page must include this warning prominently.
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.comparison_angle: Consolidation loan (10%, 7yr) vs credit cards (25%, minimum payments) vs avalanche method. Show the math of each.
page_3_debt_consolidation_loan.doorway_defense: Unique consolidation comparison content. Addresses a specific financial decision (consolidate vs don't). Not replicable by just changing the numbers — the comparison to credit card debt is the core unique value.

page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.search_intent: CALCULATOR — user wants to know the monthly payment for a $30K auto or large purchase loan at a competitive rate
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.unique_use_case: Auto loans and large purchase loans typically have lower rates than general personal loans because they may be secured. This page explains the difference between secured and unsecured loans for large purchases.
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.why_this_page_deserves_to_exist: Someone buying a $30K car at a dealership will be offered dealer financing (often 5-9%). This page gives them a benchmark to compare against. It also explains secured vs unsecured loan rates — content not on the generic calculator.
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.alternative_scenario: Dealer financing at 9% = $622.75/mo — $1,723 more over 5 years. 3-year term at 7% = $926.33/mo — saves $2,307 in interest but higher monthly.
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.risk_warning: A $30K car loan means you're paying $35,642 total — the car must be worth it. Also: if this is an unsecured personal loan at 7%, verify it's not a secured loan disguised as personal (some lenders require collateral for this amount).
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.comparison_angle: Dealer financing (9%) vs personal loan (7%) vs shorter term (3yr at 7%). Also compare to lease payments for same vehicle.
page_4_auto_large_purchase_loan.doorway_defense: Auto/large purchase context is unique. Secured vs unsecured education is unique content. Dealer financing comparison is actionable value not found elsewhere.

secondary_queries: is 20 percent apr high for personal loan
page_5_fair_credit_reality.search_intent: CALCULATOR + REALITY CHECK — user likely has fair/poor credit and wants to know what they'll actually pay
page_5_fair_credit_reality.unique_use_case: The reality check page. Shows what happens when you have lower credit — the same $20K loan costs $7,000+ more in interest than at 8%. This is the 'should I even take this loan?' page.
page_5_fair_credit_reality.why_this_page_deserves_to_exist: Many people with fair credit don't realize how much more they pay. This page doesn't just calculate — it EDUCATES and WARNS. It shows the cost of borrowing with lower credit and offers alternatives (credit improvement, smaller loan, secured options).
page_5_fair_credit_reality.alternative_scenario: Same $20K at 8% (good credit) = $405.53/mo — saves $7,461. If you improve credit from 620 to 680, you might qualify for 12-15% instead of 20%.
page_5_fair_credit_reality.risk_warning: At 20% APR, you'll pay nearly 60% of the loan amount in interest alone. This is expensive money. Consider: (1) Can you borrow less? $10K at 20% costs $264.94/mo. (2) Can you improve credit first? 6 months of credit building could save thousands. (3) Is there a secured alternative? A secured loan or co-signer may offer lower rates.
page_5_fair_credit_reality.comparison_angle: Same $20K: 8% (excellent) vs 12% (good) vs 20% (fair) vs 30% (poor). Show the brutal math — credit score affects total cost by $10,000+ on the same loan.
page_5_fair_credit_reality.doorway_defense: Not a number-swap — the entire page narrative changes for high-rate scenarios. It becomes a credit education and warning page, not just a calculator. This intent shift is unique and defensible.

cross_page_rules: Each page targets a DIFFERENT use case, intent, and audience
Page 2 ($20K at 8%) and Page 5 ($20K at 20%) use the same loan amount but TOTALLY DIFFERENT narratives — good credit benchmark vs fair credit warning
All pages link to each other contextually (not auto-generated link blocks)
No two pages share the same comparison table — each comparison is tailored to the use case
FAQs are unique per page — not templated
Titles and metas are descriptive and unique — no 'X Loan at Y%' pattern reuse
